metadata.title: One Mark. Every Move Remotion Knows.
metadata.description: A technique-showcase reel animating the Bear Brown initials mark every way Remotion can — 16:9 landscape edition. One SVG path (viewBox 7500×5000, 3:2 ratio), 20 techniques, for review: which moves earn their beat? This is Liam, in for Bear.

beats.narration_text: Hey, this is Liam, in for Bear. One mark — the Bear Brown initials. We are running it through twenty motion techniques back to back so you can judge which ones deserve to live.
Spring Entrance. The mark drops in from above on a spring — damping twenty, stiffness two hundred. Watch whether it reads as gravity or bounce.
Overshoot Spring with squash and stretch. The mark lands, compresses on impact, then rebounds past center. The squash ratio is the tell — too much and it reads cartoon.
Draw-On Stroke. stroke-dashoffset traces the single complex path, then the fill floods in. One path, one draw — watch the corners where the stroke turns.
Mask Reveal. First a clip-path wipe left to right, then a radial iris opens from center. Two reveal geometries, one mark. Which one reads as intention?
Scale Zoom — linear versus bezier. First pass is linear scale-in, mechanical and even. Second pass uses a cubic bezier — fast start, slow settle. Same destination, different feeling.
Rotation. Full turn, then a final settle. The mark's three-to-two ratio means at ninety degrees it fills the frame differently. Watch whether the proportions hold.
Skew and Shear. The mark leans forward — skewX plus a compensating scaleY so it doesn't collapse. Forward lean reads speed. Back lean reads drag. Judge the angle.
Opacity Through Blur. The mark fades in while the blur resolves — not opacity alone, but focus arriving with presence. The blur ramp is faster than the fade.
Color Interpolation — treatment beat. Ink sweeps through the terracotta spectrum and back. This is the one beat where the palette rule is intentionally broken. The violation is the demo.
Kinetic Grid. The mark tiles across the full frame in a staggered grid, then a ripple wave pulses outward from center. Works best when the mark reads at thumbnail scale.
Glitch Slices. Horizontal strips offset in opposing directions, then snap clean. The snap should feel like a correction — not a feature.
Trail Echo. The mark moves laterally, leaving fading ghost copies behind it. The trail length is the question — too short reads static, too long reads smear.
Noise Wobble. Position driven by a sine-based pseudo-noise — organic drift without a pattern. The mark never leaves frame, never repeats exactly.
Elastic Physics. Low damping, moderate stiffness — the mark oscillates past its rest point multiple times before settling. Spring config is everything here.
Card Flip — perspective rotateY. The mark flips on its Y axis, briefly showing an invisible reverse side, then lands face up. The perspective distance is the director's choice.
Shadow Play. A box shadow animated from soft-diffuse to sharp-cast, tracking a simulated light source moving overhead. Shadow length and angle tell the story.
Composer Summon. The terracotta spark fires on send, then the mark assembles as the result. Ask, then result — the brand's receipt law, applied to a logo.
Stroke Pulse. stroke-dashoffset runs in a looping heartbeat rhythm — the mark breathes in ink, then exhales back to fill. The pulse rate is the heartbeat of the brand.
Scale Breathe. A continuous subtle scale oscillation — ninety-seven to one-oh-three percent and back, looping. Barely perceptible in isolation, but unmissable in a static frame.
Exit Family. Three quick exits: shrink-spin, blur-out, mask-close. The word leaves three ways. Which one you would cut to black on — that is the judgment call.
Now you have the receipts. Take this prompt, run it on your own mark, and flag the two or three moves that actually serve the brand.
